name: Kalua Pork
steps: Season pork with soy sauce, slow-cook or simmer 45-50 minutes until fall-apart tender.
Shred and serve over cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Rub the pork with soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and liquid smoke (a stand-in for the traditional underground imu oven's smoky flavor).
Cover and cook low and slow - either in a slow cooker on low for 7-8 hours, or simmered covered on the stovetop over low heat for 45-60 minutes - until completely fork-tender.
Shred the pork, mixing it with the cooking liquid.
Serve over rice with a side of shredded cabbage - kalua pig is the centerpiece of a traditional Hawaiian luau.

name: Huli Huli Chicken
steps: Marinate chicken in soy sauce and brown sugar 15 minutes.
Grill 6-7 minutes per side, basting occasionally.
steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, brown sugar, ginger, garlic, and a splash of the reserved pineapple juice together for the marinade.
Coat the chicken and marinate at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor.
Grill over medium-high heat, 6-7 minutes per side, basting with extra marinade partway through ('huli' means 'turn' in Hawaiian, referring to flipping the chicken repeatedly while basting).
Serve with the pineapple chunks alongside.

name: Loco Moco
steps: Cook the beef patty 4-5 minutes per side.
Fry the eggs.
Serve patty and eggs over cooked rice, with a simple pan gravy if desired.
steps_detailed: Cook the rice per package directions.
Season the beef patty and cook in a skillet, 4-5 minutes per side, until done to preference. Remove.
Whisk the flour into the broth and soy sauce, add to the same pan, and simmer 2-3 minutes until thickened into a simple gravy.
Fry the eggs sunny-side up in the same pan.
Build the bowl: rice on the bottom, the beef patty on top, gravy over that, and the fried eggs crowning it all - loco moco is a beloved Hawaiian plate-lunch classic, invented in Hilo in the 1940s.

name: Ahi Poke Bowl
steps: Toss cubed tuna with soy sauce, marinate 10 minutes.
Serve over cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Only use very fresh, sushi-grade tuna for this dish, since it's eaten raw.
Toss the cubed tuna with soy sauce, sesame oil, and scallions. Marinate 10-15 minutes in the fridge.
Cook the rice per package directions.
Build the bowl with rice, the marinated tuna, and diced cucumber, topped with sesame seeds - poke has been a staple of Hawaiian cuisine for generations, well before its recent mainland popularity.

name: Teriyaki Beef
steps: Marinate beef in soy sauce and brown sugar 15 minutes.
Sear 2-3 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, brown sugar, garlic, and ginger together. Coat the beef and marinate at least 20 minutes.
Heat a splash of oil in a skillet over high heat and sear the beef, 2-3 minutes per side, until caramelized at the edges.
Top with sesame seeds before serving - teriyaki reflects Hawaii's deep Japanese immigrant influence, now a plate-lunch staple.

name: Pork and Greens (Lau Lau-Style)
steps: Brown the pork, 5-6 minutes.
Add spinach and soy sauce with 1/2 cup water, simmer 25-30 minutes until pork is tender.

ingredients_full.note: or collard greens, substitute for taro (luau) leaves

steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a heavy pot over medium-high heat and brown the pork, 5-6 minutes.
Add the ginger, soy sauce, and broth. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook 25-30 minutes until the pork is very tender.

Add the spinach in batches, letting each wilt before adding more, and cook 5 more minutes - traditional lau lau wraps pork and butterfish in taro leaves and steams the whole bundle, but this stovetop version keeps the same comforting flavor.

name: Hawaiian BBQ Short Ribs
steps: Marinate beef in soy sauce and pineapple juice 20 minutes.
Grill 3-4 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Blend or mash the pineapple chunks with their juice, then whisk with soy sauce, brown sugar, garlic, and sesame oil for the marinade.
Coat the beef and marinate at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor - pineapple's enzymes help tenderize the meat, a classic Hawaiian technique.
Grill over medium-high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until caramelized and cooked to preference.

name: Spam Musubi
steps: Pan-sear Spam slices 2-3 minutes per side.
Press rice into blocks, top with Spam, wrap with a strip of nori.
steps_detailed: Pan-sear the Spam slices, 2-3 minutes per side, until browned.
Whisk the soy sauce and brown sugar together, add to the pan in the last minute to glaze the Spam.
Cook the rice and press firmly into rectangular blocks (a musubi mold or an empty Spam can with both ends removed works well).

Top each rice block with a slice of glazed Spam, then wrap with a strip of nori to hold it together - musubi reflects Hawaii's post-WWII Spam tradition, now a beloved local snack sold at every convenience store.

name: Chicken Katsu Plate
steps: Bread chicken in flour, egg, and breadcrumbs.
Pan-fry 3-4 minutes per side, slice and serve over rice.
steps_detailed: Pound the chicken thin. Dredge in flour, dip in egg, then coat thoroughly in panko.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and fry 3-4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through.
Whisk the soy sauce and honey together for a quick dipping sauce.
Slice the chicken and serve over rice with the sauce - katsu is a defining part of the classic Hawaiian 'plate lunch,' alongside rice and macaroni salad.

name: Poke Bowl (Lunch Size)
steps: Toss tuna with soy sauce.
Serve over cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Toss the cubed tuna with soy sauce and sesame oil.
Cook the rice per package directions.
Serve the tuna over rice, topped with scallions - a lighter, quicker take on the dinner poke bowl.

name: Macaroni Salad with Chicken
steps: Toss cooled macaroni with chicken and mayonnaise.

ingredients_full.note: cooked until quite soft, then cooled

steps_detailed: Cook the macaroni a bit past al dente - authentic Hawaiian mac salad wants the pasta soft, not firm, so it absorbs the dressing well.
Cool completely, then toss with the chicken, carrot, mayonnaise, and milk.
Chill before serving - mac salad is the essential third component of any proper Hawaiian plate lunch.

name: Portuguese Sausage and Eggs
steps: Pan-sear sausage 4-5 minutes.
Fry the eggs, serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Pan-sear the sausage slices over medium heat, 4-5 minutes, until browned.
Fry the eggs in the same pan.
Serve alongside rice - a classic Hawaiian breakfast plate, reflecting the islands' Portuguese immigrant heritage.

name: Hawaiian-Style French Toast
steps: Dip bread in beaten egg, pan-fry 2-3 minutes per side.
Top with pineapple.
steps_detailed: Whisk the egg and milk together. Dip the bread slices, coating both sides well.
Pan-fry over medium heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden.
Top with pineapple chunks, toasted coconut, and a drizzle of honey - island flavors on a familiar breakfast classic, a popular brunch item across the islands.

name: Spam Musubi Bites
steps: Pan-sear Spam 2-3 minutes per side.
Press rice into small blocks, top with Spam, wrap with nori.
steps_detailed: Pan-sear the Spam with a light glaze of soy sauce, 2-3 minutes per side.
Press the rice into small blocks, top with Spam, and wrap with a strip of nori - a smaller, snack-portioned version of the classic musubi.

name: Macadamia Nut Snack
steps: Enjoy as-is or lightly toasted in a dry skillet 2-3 minutes.
steps_detailed: Toss the macadamia nuts with honey.
Toast in a dry skillet over medium-low heat, 3-4 minutes, stirring often, until fragrant and lightly caramelized.
Cool before eating - macadamias are one of Hawaii's signature crops and a beloved local snack.

name: Dried Pineapple Snack
steps: Pat pineapple dry, bake at 250F for 1.5-2 hours until chewy and dried.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 250F. Pat the pineapple very dry and slice thin if in large chunks.
Toss with a light dusting of cinnamon.
Spread on a lined baking sheet and bake 1.5-2 hours, flipping halfway, until chewy and dried.
Cool completely before eating - a naturally sweet, portable snack.

name: Haupia (Coconut Pudding)
steps: Whisk cornstarch into a little cold coconut milk, combine with remaining milk and honey.
Cook over medium heat, stirring, until thickened, about 6-8 minutes. Chill until set.
steps_detailed: Whisk the cornstarch into a splash of the cold coconut milk until smooth.
Combine with the remaining coconut milk, honey, and vanilla in a saucepan.
Cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, 6-8 minutes until thickened.
Pour into small cups and chill at least 2 hours until fully set - haupia is a traditional Hawaiian coconut pudding, a fixture at every luau.

name: Malasadas-Style Bites
steps: Make a simple sweet dough, shape into small balls, fry until golden, roll in sugar.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, egg, sugar, milk, and melted butter into a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Shape into small balls.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry 2-3 minutes per side until deeply golden and cooked through.
Roll the warm bites in sugar - malasadas arrived with Portuguese immigrants and are now a beloved Hawaiian treat, especially famous from Leonard's Bakery in Honolulu.

name: Grilled Mahi Mahi
steps: Marinate fish in soy sauce and ginger 10 minutes.
Grill or pan-sear 3-4 minutes per side.

ingredients_full.note: substitute for mahi mahi or other firm white fish

steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and lime juice together. Marinate the fish 10-15 minutes.
Grill or pan-sear over medium-high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until it flakes easily.
Top with scallions before serving - mahi mahi is one of the most popular fish on Hawaiian plate lunch menus.

name: Shrimp Scampi (Hawaiian Shrimp Truck Style)
steps: Saute shrimp with garlic 4-5 minutes until pink.
Serve over cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Melt the butter in a skillet over medium heat. Add the garlic and cook 1 minute until fragrant, being careful not to burn it - garlic shrimp trucks on Oahu's North Shore are famous for going heavy on the garlic.
Add the shrimp and cook 4-5 minutes until pink and just cooked through.
Finish with lemon juice and parsley.
Serve over rice, a North Shore shrimp truck classic.
